_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A company introduced standing desks and found that back-pain complaints dropped 30% over six months. It concluded that standing desks reduce back pain.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: During those six months, no other workplace change was made that could affect back pain.
items.ChoiceB: The company had also considered buying ergonomic chairs.
items.ChoiceC: Back pain is one of the most common complaints in office jobs.
items.ChoiceD: Employees generally said they liked the new standing desks.
items.ChoiceE: Standing for long periods can cause other health problems.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Ruling out other causes makes the desks the likely reason for the drop.
items.WhyWrongB: What was considered but not done is irrelevant.
items.WhyWrongC: Background prevalence does not bear on the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongD: Liking the desks does not show they reduced pain.
items.WhyWrongE: Other harms do not support the pain-reduction claim; if anything they cut against it.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] An ancient settlement was likely abandoned because of drought: sediment cores show a decades-long dry period beginning right around the time it was deserted.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most supports the hypothesis?
items.ChoiceA: The settlement's population had been rising before the dry period.
items.ChoiceB: Sediment cores can be dated only to within a century.
items.ChoiceC: Nearby settlements in wetter areas during the same era were not abandoned.
items.ChoiceD: The settlement contained many well-built stone houses.
items.ChoiceE: Droughts occurred repeatedly throughout the region's history.
items.WhyWrongA: Prior growth does not support drought as the cause of abandonment.
items.WhyWrongB: Loose dating weakens, not strengthens, the timing link.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. A contrast case tying abandonment specifically to drought supports the causal story.
items.WhyWrongD: Construction quality is irrelevant to why it was abandoned.
items.WhyWrongE: If droughts were common without abandonment, the link is weakened.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Films released in December win a disproportionate share of major awards. So releasing a film in December improves its chances of winning awards.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the reasoning?
items.ChoiceA: Most award voters now watch films on streaming platforms.
items.ChoiceB: Among films of similar quality, December releases win more awards than releases in other months.
items.ChoiceC: Some award-winning films are released in the spring.
items.ChoiceD: December is one of the most popular months for going to the movies.
items.ChoiceE: Studios spend far more on marketing for their December releases.
items.WhyWrongA: How voters watch is irrelevant to release timing.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Holding quality fixed isolates timing as the factor, supporting the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongC: This slightly cuts against a strict December advantage.
items.WhyWrongD: Ticket popularity does not bear on awards.
items.WhyWrongE: Marketing spend is an alternative cause — it weakens the timing claim.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A company switched to a four-day workweek and found that total employee output rose. It concluded that the shorter week boosted its workers' productivity.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Employees reported that they enjoyed having a three-day weekend.
items.ChoiceB: Total output is notoriously difficult to measure with precision.
items.ChoiceC: Throughout the trial, the employees' assigned workload and the number of staff stayed the same.
items.ChoiceD: A rival firm that kept its five-day week also saw its output rise that year.
items.ChoiceE: The four-day week reduced the company's weekly electricity costs.
items.WhyWrongA: Enjoyment does not show output rose because of the schedule.
items.WhyWrongB: Casting doubt on the measurement weakens the argument.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Holding workload and staffing fixed rules out the obvious alternative explanations, so the productivity gain is more plausibly due to the shorter week.
items.WhyWrongD: If output rose elsewhere without a shorter week, a common cause is likely — this weakens.
items.WhyWrongE: Electricity savings are irrelevant to worker productivity.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Archaeologists conclude that a coastal village was destroyed by a tsunami: a thick layer of ocean sediment lies directly above the last traces of habitation.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the archaeologists' conclusion?
items.ChoiceA: The sediment layer could only have been deposited by a large, sudden ocean wave.
items.ChoiceB: Some coastal villages have been abandoned for reasons unrelated to natural disasters.
items.ChoiceC: Tsunamis struck the region repeatedly in later centuries.
items.ChoiceD: The surrounding region was known in antiquity for its rich fishing grounds.
items.ChoiceE: The village had existed for several centuries before it was abandoned.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. If only a tsunami-scale wave could deposit that sediment, the evidence points specifically to a tsunami rather than gradual flooding or another cause.
items.WhyWrongB: Noting other causes of abandonment weakens the tsunami conclusion.
items.WhyWrongC: Later tsunamis do not establish that one destroyed this village at this time.
items.WhyWrongD: Good fishing is irrelevant to how the village ended.
items.WhyWrongE: The village's age does not bear on the cause of its destruction.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] To reduce childhood cavities, the town added fluoride to its water supply. Over the next five years, cavity rates among children fell. The fluoridation program was therefore a success.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: During those five years, children's consumption of sugary snacks and drinks did not decline.
items.ChoiceB: Cavity rates among the town's adults also fell over the same period.
items.ChoiceC: The town's dentists publicly welcomed the fluoridation program.
items.ChoiceD: Fluoride can cause tooth discoloration when consumed in very high doses.
items.ChoiceE: The town's population grew steadily throughout the five-year period.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Ruling out reduced sugar intake removes a leading alternative explanation, leaving fluoridation as the likely cause of the decline.
items.WhyWrongB: Adult rates concern a different group and do not support the childhood claim.
items.WhyWrongC: The dentists' approval does not show the program caused fewer cavities.
items.WhyWrongD: A possible harm at high doses weakens, if anything.
items.WhyWrongE: Population growth is irrelevant to the per-child cavity rate.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The new fertilizer must be what raised the farm's crop yield this year: the farm began using it in the spring and brought in a record harvest in the fall.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: A record crop is considerably more profitable than an average one.
items.ChoiceB: The new fertilizer is more expensive than the brand the farm used previously.
items.ChoiceC: Neighboring farms that used no new fertilizer also had unusually good harvests.
items.ChoiceD: The farm's soil is naturally very rich in nutrients.
items.ChoiceE: Rainfall and temperatures this year were close to the region's long-term averages.
items.WhyWrongA: Profitability is irrelevant to the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongB: Cost has no bearing on whether the fertilizer raised yield.
items.WhyWrongC: Good harvests without the fertilizer suggest a common cause — this weakens.
items.WhyWrongD: Naturally rich soil offers a competing explanation, so it weakens.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Typical weather rules out an unusually favorable season as the cause, strengthening the case for the fertilizer.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The candidate's economic plan would reduce unemployment. Voters should therefore support her over her rivals.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: None of the rival candidates has a plan that would reduce unemployment.
items.ChoiceB: Unemployment is higher now than it was a decade ago.
items.ChoiceC: The candidate has considerably more political experience than her rivals.
items.ChoiceD: Some voters are chiefly concerned with issues other than unemployment.
items.ChoiceE: The candidate's plan would also increase overall government spending.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. If only her plan reduces unemployment, the reason offered picks her out over the rivals, closing the gap to 'support her over them.'
items.WhyWrongB: The current level of unemployment does not favor her plan over others.
items.WhyWrongC: Experience is a different consideration and not the stated reason.
items.WhyWrongD: This undercuts the relevance of the stated reason, so it weakens.
items.WhyWrongE: Extra spending is a potential drawback; it does not strengthen.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Students who used the new tutoring app scored higher on the final exam than students who did not. The app clearly improves exam performance.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: The tutoring app is available free of charge to every student.
items.ChoiceB: Most students found the app straightforward to use.
items.ChoiceC: The final exam covered material drawn from the entire course.
items.ChoiceD: Students who choose to use educational apps tend to be more motivated than those who do not.
items.ChoiceE: Before the study began, app users and non-users had comparable academic records.
items.WhyWrongA: Free availability does not show the app caused higher scores.
items.WhyWrongB: Ease of use does not establish an effect on performance.
items.WhyWrongC: Exam scope does not bear on whether the app helped.
items.WhyWrongD: Greater motivation among users is an alternative cause — this weakens.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Comparable prior records rule out the self-selection worry that stronger students simply chose the app, so the score gap is better attributed to the app.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The ancient coins found at the site must have been minted locally, because they bear a symbol used by the local ruler.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: The local ruler's symbol was never placed on coins minted in other regions.
items.ChoiceB: The site was a busy trading hub visited by merchants from distant lands.
items.ChoiceC: A great many ancient coins have been recovered from the region.
items.ChoiceD: The local ruler reigned over the region for several decades.
items.ChoiceE: The coins are made of a metal that was common throughout the ancient world.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. If no foreign mint used the ruler's symbol, then a coin bearing it must have been made locally, closing the gap.
items.WhyWrongB: A trading hub makes foreign coins likelier, weakening the local-mint claim.
items.WhyWrongC: The sheer number of coins does not indicate where they were made.
items.WhyWrongD: The length of the reign is irrelevant to where the coins were minted.
items.WhyWrongE: A common metal makes a distant origin easier, if anything — it weakens.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Listening to classical music during study improves memory: in one class, students who played classical music during review recalled more terms on a quiz than students who reviewed in silence.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: Classical music is widely regarded as calming and pleasant.
items.ChoiceB: The students in both groups were preparing for the very same quiz.
items.ChoiceC: The two groups spent equal amounts of time reviewing the same set of terms.
items.ChoiceD: Some people find any background music distracting when they study.
items.ChoiceE: The music group happened to review in a quieter, more comfortable room.
items.WhyWrongA: The music's reputation does not show it improved recall.
items.WhyWrongB: Both taking the same quiz is a baseline condition, not added support.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Equal review time removes the obvious rival explanation that the music group simply studied more, isolating the music as the difference.
items.WhyWrongD: This suggests music can hurt, weakening the claim.
items.WhyWrongE: A quieter room is a confound that offers an alternative cause — it weakens.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Adding bike lanes reduced traffic accidents, since the number of accidents on streets that received new bike lanes dropped in the year after the lanes were installed.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
items.ChoiceA: On comparable streets that received no bike lanes, accident numbers held steady over the same year.
items.ChoiceB: The city spent a substantial sum of money installing the new bike lanes.
items.ChoiceC: Cyclists reported feeling considerably safer riding in the new bike lanes.
items.ChoiceD: Traffic accidents had been gradually declining citywide for the previous decade.
items.ChoiceE: The new bike lanes are used by several hundred cyclists each day.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. A stable rate on comparable lane-free streets rules out a citywide trend, so the drop is more plausibly due to the lanes.
items.WhyWrongB: Installation cost is irrelevant to the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongC: Feeling safer does not establish that accidents actually fell because of the lanes.
items.WhyWrongD: A pre-existing downward trend offers an alternative cause — this weakens.
items.WhyWrongE: Heavy usage does not show the lanes reduced accidents.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Economist: To cut bottle litter, charging a refundable deposit up front will work better than paying an equal cash reward when bottles are returned, though the sums are identical. People work harder to recover money they feel they have already lost than to gain the same amount as a bonus.
items.Stem: Which one of the following, if true, most strengthens the economist's argument?
items.ChoiceA: Cash rewards cost retailers more to administer than refundable deposits do.
items.ChoiceB: Surveys find that most shoppers say they dislike having to pay container deposits.
items.ChoiceC: In controlled trials, people returned far more containers to reclaim a prepaid deposit than to earn a reward of equal value.
items.ChoiceD: Bottle litter has been falling even in areas that use neither deposits nor rewards.
items.ChoiceE: The deposit and the reward would cost a returning consumer the same amount overall.
items.WhyWrongA: Administrative cost to retailers does not bear on which option reduces litter.
items.WhyWrongB: Disliking a deposit is not the same as returning bottles, and might even deter purchases.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. A direct behavioral comparison showing higher return rates under the deposit supports exactly the economist's prediction.
items.WhyWrongD: A decline without either scheme cuts against, not for, the deposit's special power.
items.WhyWrongE: This merely restates the premise that the sums are equal; it adds no support.
